I have considered just killing the thread but a) it felt possibly unsafe (if requests has any internal state that might be abandoned by a thread kill) and b) it can only be done from within the thread, so I would have to wait for the server to send a heartbeat so that I actually end up in my own code.

I considered creating a small wrapper class that passes along the close() but it felt like too much additional code for a small benefit. 

The thing is that I can't find any documentation on what the appropriate way to close a long polling/chunked response in Requests actually is. All of the search results on it are about the addition of the feature, and no one seems to have usage code that actually ever exits from handling a chunked response before the server is ready.

I investigated trying to indicate from the clientside that the connection is ready to be terminated (so that the server would initiate the connection drop) but I was unable to figure out how to send the zero-length termination message from requests' API. Hence why I was eager to find someone who was more familiar with the library.

If someone attaches a handler, there must be a point where it is eventually detached. The logging subsystem can't track loading/unloading of plugins by itself unfortunately, but it is necessary to provide reentrancy (at least to be able to run `pytester` in-process).

I would suggest the same approach as used in abusalimov/pytest-catchlog@4121487982a1b39937f9daa04cfc94c016e201bc:
```diff
@@ -123,13 +123,27 @@ def __init__(self, config):
+        handler = logging.StreamHandler()
+        handler.setFormatter(self.formatter)
+
+        self.handler = handler
+
+    @pytest.mark.hookwrapper
+    def pytest_runtestloop(self, session):
+        """Runs all collected test items."""
+        with catching_logs(self.handler):
+            yield  # run all the tests
+
```

For my own reference, because I'd never looked into this before: The `zope.interface` versions are broken because they want to modify the `__metaclass__` variable of the caller, which is what doesn't work on Python 3. They do this in order to arrange to be run *after* the class has been constructed and hence its bases determined, so that they can "flatten" all of the implements declarations from the parent classes into the child class, presumably for performance.

`z.c.adapts` doesn't have this issue, because it just simply assigns its argument to a value in the caller's locals. Parents aren't considered.

The reason this line was here is because SlickGrid requires rows to have a unique id property, and by default it uses the column called 'id'.  So I expect that if you run this code with a data frame that doesn't have an id column, the grid won't render and you'll see an error about the lack of a unique 'id' property.  

Here's a stack overflow discussing the id column: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/27953376/slickgrid-id-column.  

And here's the API docs for the SlickGrid DataView which requires the id: https://github.com/mleibman/SlickGrid/wiki/DataView

I think the right fix for this is to change our call to `setItems` on line 93 in this file to specify the name of a different property to use as the unique id (i.e. something that's not as likely to be used by people).  How about we call the property `slick_grid_id`.  

So what we'd need then is two changes, one here to set the `slick_grid_id` property on each row, instead of the `id` property.  So uncomment this line and change it to
```
cur_row.slick_grid_id = "row" + row_count;
```
And the other change is on line 93 to specify the property which should be used as the unique id:
```
// Not tested so you'll have to try it out
this.data_view.setItems(this.row_data, 'slick_grid_id');
``` 
This way, the only time people will run into trouble is in the unlikely case that they have a column called 'slick_grid_id'.

A bit.  Most of the issue is mapping between the values returned from the initial request and the values required to poll the operation status.  E.g., for a `CreateInstance` request, the response contains an `Operation` proto whose name/path includes the project and instance ID:

```
operations/projects/<PROJECT>/instances/<INSTANCE-ID>/locations/<LOCATION-ID>/operations/<OPERATION-ID>
```

but the value needed in the status-checking request isn't supposed to have them:

```
operations/locations/<LOCATION-ID>/operations/<OPERATION-ID>
```

The version for V2 `Cluster` is similar.

In this example, the responsive grid sizes are specified as a number of columns for each breakpoint range (small, medium, and large). The key thing to remember is that small windows have 4 columns, medium have 8, and large have 12. So these three numbers can be interpreted as:

* At small sizes, this takes 4/4 columns (full-width)
* At medium sizes, this takes 8/8 columns (full-width)
* At large sizes, this takes 4/12 columns (quarter-width)

The same sizes could be specified (perhaps more intelligibly) as percentages:

```html
<k-grid-item sizes="100, 100, 25" percentage>
```

Judging by your last comment on the main thread you may have already realized what I'm about to write but just for completeness let me elaborate further on this.

The refactored `JournalDB` works very much like the `BatchDB` in the sense that it does not write to the underlying db until some condition is met. This condition could be leaving a `with` statement or calling an explicit API. In the current design, you have to explicitly call `persist()` in order to write to the underlying database.

Before you call `persist` all changes (`set` / `delete`) go into the active changeset. Notice that there is *always* an active changeset where changes end up in.

However, you can build a stack of changesets if you explicitly call `record`. E.g.

```
changeset_1 = db.record()
db.set(a, 1)
changeset_2 = db.record()
db.set(a, 2)
changeset_3 = db.record()
db.delete(b)
db.set(c, 1)
```
At this point you'll end up with a stack of changesets that looks like this:

```
root_changeset
changeset_1
  sets a to 1
changeset_2
  sets a to 2
changset_3
  deletes b
  sets c to 1
```

Calling `commit()` or `discard(changeset_id)` will simply modify how this stack looks like but again, won't do any changes to the underlying db.

For instance, we could call `discard(changeset_3)` and would end up with this:

```
root_changeset
changeset_1
  sets a to 1
changeset_2
  sets a to 2
```

Or we could call `commit(changeset_3)` and would end up with this

```
root_changeset
changeset_1
  sets a to 1
changeset_2
  sets a to 2
  deletes b
  sets c to 1
```

Notice that `commit()` and `discard(changeset_id)` are called as part of the `Computation` handling. E.g. if a computation fails for some reason we discard the changes.

The `persist()` part on the other hand is called on the very end of the transaction processing (when all the computations are done) that's why there is only a single call to `persist()` and I *think* this should be sound.

Because the db of a trie always stores `db[keccak(val)] = val`, having different key/values in the wrapped and cached db would mean finding two different pre-images with the same sha3 hash. I don't know if there's only one right thing to do in that situation, but leaving the old one there when pruning the new one doesn't seem insane (the only cost being a little extra database usage). I also don't see anything incorrect about deleting always, but it causes a performance hit:

1. We carry around a lot more deletes to try to run on the wrapped db
2. We would now have to test if the key exists before every delete operation on `wrapped_db`

I could go either way, and I don't think it's hard to change later. Thoughts?

```Python
if supported_api_version(ResourceType.MGMT_NETWORK, min_api='2016-09-01'): 
   register_cli_argument('network nic create', 'private_ip_address_version', help='The private IP address version to use.', default=IPVersion.ipv4.value) 
else:
   register_cli_argument('network nic create', 'private_ip_address_version', ignore_type)
```
Or this:
```Python
with VersionConstraint(ResourceType.MGMT_NETWORK, min_api='2016-09-01') as c: 
    c.register_cli_argument('network nic create', 'private_ip_address_version', help='The private IP address version to use.', default=IPVersion.ipv4.value) 
```

My preference would be for the second approach.

I had a look at this and it's not that easy. I agree that using the buffer interface is a bit more efficient. I ran some tests and for a process that is around 2GB in size, the largest block is almost 300mb so we use that much additional memory. If we can avoid that, it would be better.

Now, using the buffer / memory view interface is not that intuitive. For example, internally, Yara reuses the same buffer to store data for additional blocks. That means if you do

```
block1 = memoryview(iterator.next())
block2 = memoryview(iterator.next())
```

then accessing block1 will give you data from the second block.

On top of that, the memoryview is a bit harder to use in Python. Iterating over it gives you individual bytes which means if you do ```"ABC" in memoryview(it.next())```, it will always be false. The way to fix this is to do

```
"ABC" in "".join(memoryview(block1))
```

but that will then of course create another copy and you don't win anything by using the buffer interface.

I came up with the following compromise (but now that I have implemented it I think it's not worth it):

I have both, the buffer interface and a data() method on the returned memory block. The data() returns a python string, the buffer interface a view of the memory. In theory, using the buffer should be faster and more memory efficient.

Now, I have actually done some testing and it turns out that the memory use does not increase much when using the python string. Some results - reading 2266 blocks of the ~2gb process and searching for "abc": ![memory_usage](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7632102/31176676-f71dd6b4-a913-11e7-99b9-297a9ee7a599.png) 

Note that the data() interface (python string) is more efficient overall but both max out at ~350mb. The biggest block is 300mb so neither interface makes a copy here, Python is smart enough to somehow reuse the buffers regardless of which interface is chosen.

On top of that, the buffer interface is much slower in some cases. For this use case, "abc" in "".join(memoryview(block)) for all blocks, the buffer interface uses 45s while "abc" in data() just takes 1.5s.

I suggest we leave the buffer interface in place, it's nice to be able to do

```
with open(file, "wb") as fd:
  fd.write(block)
```
What do you think?

p.s. the code as it is works for both, Python 2 and 3

Okay, I am up way too late, but finally thought about this some more.

I agree that for step != 2, you would need ``padding = N % step``.

I think you also need all for loops involving the padded region to be modified
from:
```c
    for (k = 0; k < padding && j < F; ++k, ++j)
        sum += filter[j] * input[N-1];
```
to:
```c
    for (k = 0; k < padding && j < F; ++k, ++j)
        sum += filter[j] * input[N-padding+k];
```
This second form is equivalent to the first when ``step = 2``.

The following example should illustrate that it is the desired behaviour for
step > 2:
assume ``step = 3`` and ``N = 8``,
then ``padding = 2`` and the periodized array indices into input are:
0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 : 7 6
The second for loop form causes the filter overlapping on the left to reach
the value at index 6 before the value at index 7 as illustrated above.  The
first for loop form would acces index 7 twice instead.

I'm not sure this properly captures all radio implementations.  For example, while the CC2538 may be able to carry two shorts for every extended, other implementations may be able to carry four shorts for every extended.  I know some radios will hash the extended down to 2 bytes, meaning that the overhead for each short and extended entry is the same.

I think we should consider the following approach:  Whenever we release an entry in the overflow state, first try to add as many pending shorts as we can to the table then, if there is still space available, try to add as many extended addresses to the table.  If after the adds there are no more pending addresses, then we are no longer overflowing and can enable source match again.

I think I should clarify what I mean.

ATM (as of 3cb5d526f511707d4b4d0fa2840aa60d1cc861c6), the initiator task is conceptually managing from `1` transfer up to `len(state.routes.available_routes)` transfers. To make matters simpler, the current implementation cancels the latest transfer and starts a new one. That allows us to reuse the state object and logic from the same module.

The goal #1091 is to allow *multiple* transfers to happen simultaneously, we can imagine a worst case were more than one of the transfers fail with a refund. If the refunds are valid we want to reveal the secret to improve liquidity (we probably want to pay zero fees for this, but lets neglect this for now), this is in interest of the payer since it involves liquidity of **two** channels that he is participating.

To allow multiple transfers, we need to keep a list of the current pending transfers. This means for each transfer the attributes `transfer`, `message`, `route`, `secretrequest`, and `revealsecret` from the [initiator](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/3cb5d526f511707d4b4d0fa2840aa60d1cc861c6/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/state.py#L47-L57) must be saved.

My proposal is to make a new state object that contains these fields, this state object will represent a single transfer started by the initiator, all logic to interact with this state object should be isolated into a single module, and then the current InitiatorState would sub-dispatch the state changes to the appropriate transfer task. When a refund is received for the current active transfer a new one should be started *along side* the current one, this allows the protocol to complete for the one that received the refund.

The proposed change allows multiple in-transit transfers, so that we can reveal the secret individually, and it also sets up the road for a better [cancel route](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/3cb5d526f511707d4b4d0fa2840aa60d1cc861c6/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/initiator.py#L205). The current implementation  is sub-optimal because it just drops the current transfer and start a new one, instead it should let both transfers race and finish the first one that sends a secret request (IOW, we don't want to cancel a route, but to retry with a new route after a timeout).

The plan executor and step executors are not responsible for reporting errors that they see unless the errors arose internally within those components. For example, the step executor will report any errors that come from pre or post-step events, as well as errors that can occur when handling `RegisterResourceOutputs`.

It's hard to reason about who is responsible for reporting what errors, but in general with this PR I tried to keep error reporting away from the core execution engine as much as possible. In this particular case, when we see an `Error` coming from a SourceIterator, the source iterator implementation itself has already reported the error and we don't need to do so again.
